## Cumulative Lag Calculation

Each link in the chain is **sequential** — each step can only begin after the previous one completes. I'll sum them cumulatively from January 1:

| Step | Lag | Cumulative | Calendar Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rate → mortgage rates | 0.5 mo | **0.5 mo** | ~Jan 15 |
| Mortgage rates → applications drop | 1–1.5 mo | **~2 mo** | ~Mar 1 |
| Fewer mortgages → housing starts decline | 3 mo | **~5 mo** | ~Jun 1 |
| Housing decline → construction employment drop | 2 mo | **~7 mo** | ~Aug 1 |
| Employment drop → reduced spending | 1 mo | **~8 mo** | ~Sep 1 |
| Reduced spending → GDP contraction | 3 mo | **~11 mo** | ~Dec 1 |
| GDP contraction → inflation reduction | 6 mo | **~17 mo** | ~Jun next year |

**Total chain: ~16.5–17 months.**

## What's happened by July 1 (6 months)?

By month 6, only the first three links have completed — mortgage rates adjusted, applications dropped, and housing starts are just beginning to decline. The chain hasn't yet reached **any employment, spending, GDP, or inflation effects**. Those require another ~11 months.

- **Elapsed:** 6 months (~35% of the chain)
- **Remaining:** ~10–11 months before inflation actually responds

## Answer: **(B)**

6 months elapsed, approximately 10–12 months remain — the chain has barely started affecting real economic variables.

The politician's criticism at the 6-month mark reflects a common error: treating the rate hike as though it should produce immediate inflation results, when the transmission mechanism is a long sequential chain where each effect must propagate through the next. At 6 months, the policy has only reached the housing-starts stage — it hasn't even touched employment, consumer spending, GDP, or inflation yet.
